title: BUG: Segfault/stack-overflow from initializing `numpy.random._mt19937.MT19937` with recursive list
author: devdanzin
body:
### Describe the issue:

Initializing `numpy.random._mt19937.MT19937` with a recursive list in an ASAN Python build leads to a segfault, which ASAN says comes from a stack-overflow. Confirmed to also affect `PCG64`, `Philox` etc.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy.random._mt19937

t = []
t.append(t)
numpy.random._mt19937.MT19937(t)
```

### Error message:

```shell
Thread 1 "python" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.

799     ../../../../src/libsanitizer/sanitizer_common/sanitizer_common_interceptors.inc: No such file or directory.
```

### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
2.2.5
3.13.3+ experimental free-threading build (heads/3.13:d8b90117024, Apr 21 2025, 15:20:00) [GCC 11.4.0]
```

### Runtime Environment:

```

                      'found': ['SSSE3',

                                    'AVX512_KNL',
                                    'AVX512_KNM',
                                    'AVX512_SKX',

 {'architecture': 'Haswell',
  'filepath': '/home/danzin/venvs/3.13_upstream_venv/lib/python3.13t/site-packages/numpy.libs/libscipy_openblas64_-6bb31eeb.so',
  'internal_api': 'openblas',
  'num_threads': 8,
  'prefix': 'libscipy_openblas',
  'threading_layer': 'pthreads',
  'user_api': 'blas',
  'version': '0.3.28'}]
```

### Context for the issue:

I have been fuzzing Numpy using [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) by @vstinner. I realize these crashes are unlikely to be triggered in normal usage and therefore might be of low priority.

The fuzzing was done with an ASAN free-threading clang build and not confirmed on a GILfull non-sanitizer GCC build yet.

comments.author: seberg
comments.body:
> Maybe related to https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/28784?

No, but IMO, please do start to bunch up these clearly miniscule issues.  FWIW, while this can be worked around in NumPy but it is probably again a Cython or Python issue.

Not that I would consider it a real issue.  It's easy to crash the interpreter with malicious recursions.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
> No, but IMO, please do start to bunch up these clearly miniscule issues.

Sure, will do. Thanks for the guidance!
